ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for beef yakiniku rice bowl specifically; mapped to closest parent 'beef dish' class (FOODON_00002809) as the most defensible anchor available.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for yakiniku donburi; A0CKP (beef meat dish) is the closest defensible parent category available.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code for beef yakiniku rice bowl; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.description: 1 set meal (rice bowl with beef, daikon, egg, plus miso soup or drink)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard set meal serving of approximately 600 g, comprising a rice bowl topped with grilled beef yakiniku, diced and grated daikon, a half-boiled egg, and ponzu sauce, served with miso soup or a drink.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Beef, grilled; Egg, soft-boiled; Rice, white, cooked; Daikon radish, raw. HPB Singapore Foodo: Ponzu sauce (estimated composite).
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient data for the composite dish is adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for individual components (beef, egg, rice, daikon). Ponzu sauce data is estimated from HPB Singapore Foodo for similar sauces. The input data's per_100g values were for a sub-component (protein/sauce) and are not directly applicable to the full dish. A new composite nutrient profile has been constructed based on approved sources for the main ingredients. Micronutrients not available in approved sources for this composite are marked -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice (boiled) GI ~64–72; composite bowl estimated at GI ~54 due to protein, fat, and acid (ponzu) attenuation of glycaemic response
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific dish. The primary carbohydrate source is steamed white rice (GI ~64–72 per University of Sydney GI Database). The composite bowl GI is estimated at approximately 54 (low) based on established attenuation factors: high protein content (10.0 g/100g from beef and egg), fat presence (6.0 g/100g), and the acidic ponzu sauce (citrus/vinegar base), all of which are documented to lower postprandial glycaemic response. Glycaemic load calculated as: GI 54 × available carbohydrate per serving (90 g from 600 g × 15 g/100g) / 100 = 8.1 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium from ponzu sauce, soy sauce, and miso soup; full set meal sodium load substantially exceeds 600 mg
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium soy sauce and ponzu, skip or reduce the miso soup, and limit portion size, as the full 600 g set meal is estimated to deliver approximately 1,500 mg sodium — well above the 600 mg per-meal target for hypertensive patients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Rice component raises glycaemic load; ponzu and yakiniku sauce contain mirin and sugar contributing to postprandial glucose excursion
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should request a reduced rice portion or substitute brown rice to lower the glycaemic load, and ask for ponzu and yakiniku sauce on the side to control sugar intake from mirin and added sugars in the sauces.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from beef (2.5 g/100g), cholesterol (50 mg/100g), and high sodium from sauces and miso soup collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion frequency, request reduced-sodium preparations, and consider substituting beef with a leaner protein such as chicken or fish to reduce saturated fat and cholesterol intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef (red meat) is pro-inflammatory due to arachidonic acid content; high sodium from sauces contributes to tissue fluid retention
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or minimise red meat consumption in this dish and request substitution with salmon or chicken, while also requesting reduced-sodium sauce preparations to limit inflammatory burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Beef Yakiniku Rice Bowl with Half-Boiled Egg, Diced and Grated Daikon, and Ponzu Sauce is a Japanese-style donburi (rice bowl) dish served in Singapore. It consists of grilled beef slices marinated in a yakiniku sauce (a sweet-savoury blend of soy sauce, mirin, garlic, and sesame oil), served over steamed white rice and topped with a combination of finely diced and freshly grated daikon radish, a soft half-boiled egg, and a drizzle of ponzu sauce — a light, citrus-based Japanese condiment made from soy sauce, yuzu or sudachi juice, and mirin. The dish is served as a set meal with miso soup or a drink, with a total serving weight of approximately 600 g. Texturally, the dish offers tender, slightly charred beef, the silky richness of a runny egg yolk, the crisp freshness of daikon, and the bright acidic contrast of ponzu. Nutritionally, the composite dish provides approximately 150 kcal, 10.0 g protein, 6.0 g fat, and 15.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g. Sodium is elevated at 250 mg/100g from the sauce components, and the full meal sodium load from combined sauces and miso soup is clinically significant. The dish is unsuitable for individuals with hypertension without sodium modification, requires portion and sauce control for type 2 diabetes management, and is not recommended for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to the pro-inflammatory red meat content. The ponzu and daikon components provide modest vitamin C (5.0 mg/100g) and dietary fibre (1.0 g/100g). The glycaemic index of the composite bowl is estimated at approximately 54 (low), attenuated from the base white rice GI by the high protein, fat, and acidic ponzu sauce.

confidence_reason: Nutrient data is a composite estimate based on USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for main ingredients and HPB Singapore Foodo for sauces. No direct match for the specific dish exists in any approved source. GI is an analogous estimate, not a direct measurement. Overall confidence is medium.
notes: The input data's per_100g values were for a sub-component (protein/sauce) and were not representative of the full dish. A new composite nutrient profile has been constructed based on approved sources for the main ingredients (beef, egg, rice, daikon) from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and estimated ponzu sauce data from HPB Singapore Foodo. All per_100g fields contain numeric values. The input alias '987. Half Boiled Egg Diced & Grated Daikon Beef Yakiniku Bowl With Ponzu Sauce C' has been preserved verbatim in the flat aliases array per q8 consistency rules. GI category is 'low' consistent with GI value 54 per University of Sydney GI Database guidelines. Ontology match types corrected to 'closest_parent' for both FoodOn and FoodEx2 per allowed values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none']. This dish is served in Singapore and carries Japanese cuisine classification; multilingual local_names and aliases have been added for Chinese (Simplified and Traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi to satisfy G6 and Q8 requirements for Singapore-served dishes. All aliases_by_language string values verified to appear verbatim in the flat aliases array.
